coredata.dc:description: This work focused on the mechanism of photogenerated hole (h+) mediating the generation of active species in visible-light-assisted photocatalytic peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. Ferrous gluconate derived iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) were synthesized to investigate the vital role of h+ in the process of photocatalytic PMS activation for acetaminophen degradation by quenching experiments, ESR spectra, and DFT calculations. Within 20 minutes, Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system can achieve a 100 % degradation of acetaminophen (15 μM) with low-addition of PMS (0.2 mM) and Fe-CDs (12 μg/L). The Fe3+ sites on Fe-CDs reduce the activation energy for the generation of SO5 −• from PMS, increase the concentration and utilization of h+ in the system, and make the direct activation of PMS by h+ be the dominant pathway to mediate the generation of •OH and 1O2. This work provides mechanistic insights for further research on h+ mediated regulation of active species in photocatalytic PMS activation process for micropollutants removal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work focused on the mechanism of photogenerated hole (h+) mediating the generation of active species in visible-light-assisted photocatalytic peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation. Ferrous gluconate derived iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) were synthesized to investigate the vital role of h+ in the process of photocatalytic PMS activation for acetaminophen degradation by quenching experiments, ESR spectra, and DFT calculations. Within 20 minutes, Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system can achieve a 100 % degradation of acetaminophen (15 μM) with low-addition of PMS (0.2 mM) and Fe-CDs (12 μg/L). The Fe3+ sites on Fe-CDs reduce the activation energy for the generation of SO5 −• from PMS, increase the concentration and utilization of h+ in the system, and make the direct activation of PMS by h+ be the dominant pathway to mediate the generation of •OH and 1O2. This work provides mechanistic insights for further research on h+ mediated regulation of active species in photocatalytic PMS activation process for micropollutants removal.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation by heterogeneous photocatalysts have emerged as a promising strategy due to its strong practicality, recyclability, and reasonable cost-effectiveness [1,2]. Photogenerated carriers can activate PMS to generate free radicals and non-free radicals under visible light irradiation, and effectively remove organic pollutants in the environment [3–5]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have shown potential as suitable PMS photoactivators because of their small size effect, good photo-response and excellent carrier migration [6]. Han et al. used CDs modified with phenylhydrazine as PMS photoactivators to degrade methylene blue (MB) and achieved complete degradation within 2 minutes [7,8]. Ming et al. loaded CDs onto polymer carbon nitride (PCN) to enhance light absorption and the separation of charge carriers, achieving effective degradation of bisphenol A (BPA) [9]. Due to the spin density and charge distribution characteristics of CDs, the number of catalytic active centers in CDs is insufficient, making it difficult to achieve satisfactory results even with high dosage of CDs in the catalytic process of activating PMS [10–12]. Therefore, the study of CDs for photocatalytic PMS activation under visible light required further improvement in catalytic efficiency [13].

Heteroatom doping is widely used to improve the internal electronic environment and considered as an effective strategy to enhance the catalytic performance of CDs [14]. Metal and non-metal element doping are two commonly used modification methods [15–17]. Compared with non-metal element doping, transition metal elements doping (such as Fe, Co, Mn, Cu) can enable them to chelate with CDs to form better structures. This is attributed to the variable valence states and unoccupied orbitals of transition metal elements, which enable the electronic structure of CDs to be well modulated [18–20]. In addition, metal element doping can alter the electronic structure of CDs, thereby modulating the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy gap or bandgap of CDs [21]. Metal ions introduction can also enhance the conductivity of CDs, thereby improving the electron transfer efficiency on CDs [12,16,22]. With higher efficiency in utilizing active sites, Fe-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) have been extensively studied. However, general Fe-CDs synthesis require the addition of iron sources, carbon sources, and ethylenediamine, which poses a problem of not being green and simple in synthesis [23]. Owing to the advantages of simple and green synthesis of CDs, glucose is usually used as a precursor for the synthesis of CDs. To improve the catalytic performance of CDs by introducing iron sources while retaining the simplicity and greenness of synthesis, ferrous gluconate (FG) seems to be a satisfactory precursor for the synthesis of Fe-CDs. FG (Fe(C6H11O7)2• 2 H2O) is commonly used as food nutrient enhancer and drug [24]. Because of high bioavailability and stability under oxidation, FG can be a better iron source than other ionic ferrous salts [25]. Fe-CDs derived from FG may obtain a more reasonable distribution of Fe atoms.

However, in most studies on the activation of PMS by photocatalysts, the main mechanisms for the activation of PMS by photocatalysts are generally considered as the activation of PMS mediated by photogenerated electrons (e−) and the evolution of active species. Researchers tend to explain the dominant role of free radicals (such as •OH and SO4 •−) and non-free radicals (such as 1O2, surface-bound oxides, surface-bound free radicals, electron transfer and hypervalent metals) through photogenerated electron transfer pathways [26–29]. Meanwhile, some studies have also pointed out the contribution of photogenerated h+. However, the role of photogenerated holes in the photocatalytic PMS activation for the generation of active species was always been overlooked [30–32]. Generally, it is considered that h+ exhibited limited contribution to degradation through two pathways. First, h+ can oxidize H2O into •OH and achieve the evolution of other active species (O2 •−, 1O2, etc.) [33]. Second, h+ with strong oxidation ability can also directly oxidize pollutants. Despite the abundance of photogenerated holes, researchers always attributed the generation of reactive oxygen species to electron transfer process. For instance, Fang et al. used Mo2C/α-Fe2O3 for photocatalytic PMS activation with h+ as the primary active species confirmed by radical trapping experiment [34]. However, the mechanisms of active species generation had been focused on electron transfer in the heterojunctions. Similarly, Yang et al. reported significant inhibition of h+ on PMS activation using different types of nitrogen doped CDs and carboxyl regulated CDs photocatalysts, but did not further analyze the mediation role of h+ on active species [35]. Currently, there is still a lack of research on the mechanism of h+ mediated mechanism of producing active species in photoactivated PMS systems. The dominant mechanism of h+ in regulating the generation of active species has been overlooked.

Herein, Fe-CDs were synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal method by using FG as the precursor for acetaminophen (ACE) degradation by visible-light photocatalytic PMS activation. The reactive oxygen species were investigated through quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR). The process of Fe(Ⅱ), Fe(Ⅲ), and Fe(Ⅳ) valence state transition at Fe3+ sites on the surface of Fe-CDs on the effect of PMS activation were also investigated. The molecular structure and Fukui function of ACE were calculated by density functional theory (DFT). Moreover, the adsorption and activation energies of CDs and Fe-CDs for PMS were also calculated. In addition, density of states (DOS) and Fermi level (Ef) were also calculated to discuss the possible mechanism. The mediating role of h+ on the non-free radical pathway dominated by 1O2 and the free radical pathway dominated by •OH were explained. The new insights on the h+ dominated pathway and mechanism in the visible-light-assisted PMS activation process will provide improvement for future research in Fenton-like reactions.

Ferrous gluconate (FG, 98 %, Aladdin Industrial Corporation), acetaminophen (ACE, 99 %, Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd), peroxymonosulfate (PMS, 42∼46 % KHSO5 basis, Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd), glucose (99 %, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (Na2S2O3•5 H2O, 99 %, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, 99 %, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99 %, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), sodium persulfate (PDS, ≥98 %, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), ammonium acetate (≥98 %, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd). All the chemicals in this research were of analytical grade and need no further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment.

As shown in Fig. 1a, Fe-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal process. Firstly, 3.0 g of FG was dissolved in 60 mL of ultrapure water, stirred for 10 minutes to obtain the FG solution and used as a precursor for Fe-CDs. The precursor solution was transferred to a 100 mL teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 200°C for 6 h. The reactor was gradually cooled down to room temperature. The product was further purified by 0.22 μm aqueous filter membranes. Then the Fe-CDs were got through a dialysis method (MWCO = 500 Da, 2d, change water every 6 hours). Possible mechanism of the synthesis of Fe-CDs has been discussed in Text S1. For characterization, the brown powders obtained from Fe-CDs solution after freeze-drying for 2 days. CDs were synthesized by the same method except that FG was changed to glucose (dialysis method, MWCO = 1000 Da, 2d, change water every 6 hours).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of catalyst samples were recorded on a Themis Z (3.2) aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscopy. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out on Japan Rigaku Mini flex600 X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation at a scanning rate of 2° min−1 to investigate the crystalline structure of the as-prepared photocatalyst. The Raman spectra were carried out on a HORIBA JY LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer equipped with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Element compositions and chemical states of the samples were obtained on an AXIS-Ultra (Kratos, Manchester, England) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) by using Al Kα as radiance source at 15 kV and 15 mA. Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT−IR) was obtained on iS10 FT−IR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) with the scanning range from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. UV−visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV−vis DRS) were recorded in the range of 200−800 nm with a SHIMADZU UV3600 spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere attachment. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was evaluated by F-7000 PL (Hitachi, Japan) at the emission wavelength of 360 nm. Transient-state PL spectra were recorded with a single photon counting spectrometer from (Edinburgh Instrument, FLS 980) with 1 μs pulse lamp as the excitation. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of radical spin-trapped were examined on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer by spin-trap reagent DMPO in water and methanol. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, ASAP2460) surface area was calculated according to the nitrogen adsorption desorption isotherm and the Barrett- Joyner-Halenda (BJH) was used to obtain the pore size distribution of samples through adsorption isotherm branch. All photo-electrochemical characteristic for the catalysts were performed by a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua Ltd., China).

The photocatalytic experiment was conducted under visible light, using a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter. In degradation experiment, 1.2 mg catalyst was dispersed into ACE solution (15 μM, 100 mL). Before light irradiation and the addition of PMS, the mixed solution was vigorously stirred in dark for 30 min to attain the adsorption and desorption equilibrium between catalyst and ACE. The distance between the light source and the liquid surface was 15 cm. The average light intensity was measured to be 0.294 W. Then, 0.2 mM PMS was quickly added to the solution to initiate the photocatalytic experiment under visible light irradiation. At designated time intervals, 1 mL samples were taken and mixed with 20 μL sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3, 100 mM) to quench the reaction. The samples were filtered by 0.22 μm aqueous filter membranes.

The concentration of ACE in the solution phase was analyzed by 1260 Infinity II high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent, CA, USA) equipped with Agilent Zorbax SB-Aq (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 m) column. The detailed conditions of the detection method were displayed in Table S1. ACE degradation intermediates were studied using an UltiMate 3000 series ultrahigh performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry system (UHPLC-MS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA, USA) with a Zorbax Rx-C18 column. The detailed conditions of the detection method were displayed in Table S2. PMS concentration was quantified per a spectrophotometric method using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV–1800, Shimadzu, Japan). Detailed detection methods were displayed in Text S2.

For the ACE structure and Fukui function, all density functional theory (DFT) computations were conducted using the Gaussian16 software package (revision A.0.31). Geometric optimizations and single-point calculations were carried out with the M06–2X functional and the Ahlrichs def2-TZVP basis set. The Fukui function values in the real space were generated by Multiwfn 3.8 (dev), and all graphics were rendered via the Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) 1.9.3. Within the framework of the generalized gradient approximation (GGA), all spin-polarized density functional theory (DFT) computations were executed using the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) formula. To describe the ionic cores, the projector augmented wave (PAW) potential was employed, and the valence electrons were considered using a plane wave basis set with an energy cutoff of 450 eV. Additionally, van der Waals interactions were taken into consideration using the DFT-D3 method proposed by Grimme. The electronic energy is regarded as self-consistent when the energy deviation is less than 10−5 eV. A geometry optimization is considered to have converged if the energy alteration is smaller than 0.02 eV Å−1. The cluster was placed within a cubic supercell of 22 × 22 × 19 Å. During the relaxation process, a Gamma-centered grid with a 1 × 1 × 1 configuration in the Brillouin zone was utilized. A vacuum layer of 15 Å was typically incorporated onto the surface to eliminate the spurious interactions between periodic images. Spin-polarized computations were carried out for this calculation. For each elementary step, the Gibbs reaction free energy change (ΔG) is defined as the disparity between the free energies of the initial and final states and is given by the expression:
ΔG = ΔE + ΔZPE + TΔS
where ΔE is the reaction energy of reactant and product molecules adsorbed on catalyst surface, obtained from DFT calculations; ΔZPE and ΔS are the change in zero-point energies and entropy due to the reaction.

To further investigate the structure of the photocatalyst and analyze the elemental valence states, XPS spectra of CDs and Fe-CDs were obtained. XPS survey and elemental compositions of CDs and Fe-CDs were shown in Fig. S4 and Table S2, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum could be fitted into three peaks (284.80 eV, 286.70 eV and 287.47 eV), respectively assigned to CC/C−C, C−O and CO (Fig. 2d). In O 1 s spectra, peaks at 531.62 eV, 533.30 eV and 529.80 eV were respectively assigned to CO, C−O, hydroxyl groups (Fig. 2e). Compared to CDs, Fe-CDs had more −OH structures, which attributed to the abundant −OH groups in FG. The main peaks of Fe 2p1/2, Fe 2p3/2 appeared at 727.61 eV and 713.88 eV which assigned to Fe3+ and typical peaks of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 at 722.73 eV and 711.17 eV which assigned to Fe2+ (Fig. 2f). It is worth noting that Fe3+ had the highest content in Fe-CDs, indicating that Fe3+ was the main state of Fe-CDs. UV–Vis–DRS (Fig. 2g) showed a similar absorption peak at 280 nm, which may be contributed by the π-π* transition of sp2 conjugated structure [41]. Compared with CDs, the absorption edge of Fe-CDs was red-shifted from 535 nm to 634 nm, which attributed to the 2Eg → 2 T2 g transitions from O to Fe atoms. In detail, the presence of Fe−O contributes significantly to the redshift of the absorption edge. On the Fe−O bond, the charge transfer from O to Fe causes a red shift in the absorption edge, corresponding to the transition from 2Eg to 2 T2 g. In addition, −OH on Fe-CDs (Fig. 2e) can also narrow the gap between HOMO and LUMO, leading to a spectral red-shift [42]. Also, the d-d transitions of doped Fe ions increased the absorption of Fe-CDs in the visible and near-infrared regions [42]. This indicated that the introduction of Fe helped to improve the visible light absorption for Fe-CDs. The bandgap energy (Eg) of Fe-CDs and CDs were calculated by Kubelka-Munk function by UV−Vis data. As shown in Fig. S5, the bandgap energy of Fe-CDs (Eg = 1.86 eV) was lower than that of CDs (Eg = 2.81 eV), indicating that the Fe modification led to a narrower bandgap of Fe-CDs. The VB−XPS spectrum (Fig. S6) showed that the VB of CDs and Fe-CDs were 2.76 eV and 1.64 eV, respectively. Time resolved fluorescence (TRF) spectrum was used to investigate the diffusion and migration abilities of charge carriers in CDs and Fe-CDs under photoexcitation (Fig. 2h). Longer fluorescence lifetime indicated that Fe-CDs had better carrier separation ability and photostability, stronger light absorption ability and better photogenerated charges ability to participate in photocatalysis [43]. Considering the structure of Fe-CDs, the large I
D
/I
G number in Raman spectrum, narrowed bandgap and longer fluorescence lifetime could also be assigned to the oxygen related groups introduced with FG.

Photo-electrochemical experiments were used to further investigate the bandgap and electron transfer ability of CDs and Fe-CDs. The photochemical experiment was used to further investigate electron transfer ability of CDs and Fe-CDs. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was used to investigate the separation ability of photogenerated charge carriers in CDs and Fe-CDs ( Fig. 3a) [44]. CDs exhibited a high-intensity emission peak at 444 nm, while Fe-CDs did not have clear emission peak. This indicated that CDs undergo significant photogenerated carrier recombination under light excitation, while Fe-CDs significantly suppresses the recombination of photogenerated carriers, providing conditions for the accumulation and full utilization of photogenerated holes to mediate active species [45]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots also indicated that Fe-CDs had lower electron transfer resistance and better carrier migration ability than CDs (Fig. 3b) [38]. Mott-Schottky plots showed positive slopes for CDs and Fe-CDs, revealing the characteristics of n-type semiconductors [46]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the flat band potentials of CDs and Fe-CDs were −0.12 eV and −0.09 eV, respectively, which were close to the conduction band positions calculated from valence band spectra and UV−Vis data. The electronic band structures of CDs and Fe-CDs were shown in Fig. 3d. Tafel curve indicated that Fe-CDs had a higher corrosion current, suggesting that Fe-CDs had better electron transfer and catalytic abilities than CDs (Fig. S7) [47]. Tafel slope of CDs was calculated to be 310.94 mV dec−1, which was 14.97 times higher than Fe-CDs (20.77 mV dec−1) (Fig. 3e), indicating that Fe-CDs had better catalytic activity [48]. In addition, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) showed that the current density of Fe-CDs was higher and further significantly increased under visible light irradiation (Fig. 3f). This demonstrated the excellent photocarriers transfer ability of Fe-CDs and their ability to effectively promote the generation of photogenerated carriers under visible light excitation [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation performance of the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system
Comparison of various processes for ACE removal
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiencies of Fe-CDs and CDs toward photocatalytic PMS activation were evaluated by ACE degradation ( Fig. 4a-4b). The degradation efficiency of Vis (0.15 %), PMS-only (0.42 %), PMS/Vis (6.04 %), Fe-CDs/Vis (1.14 %) and CDs/Vis (1.55 %) systems was relatively low, which indicated that under the activation of light, neither PMS nor the catalyst can effectively generate active species to participate in degradation. Compared to CDs/PMS system, the degradation efficiency of Fe-CDs/PMS system can reach 40.86 %, indicating that Fe-CDs was more effective for PMS activation. Significantly, Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system reached 100 % degradation efficiency of ACE within 16 minutes under visible light irradiation. However, the CDs/PMS/Vis system can only achieve 9.46 % of ACE degradation. This suggested that Fe-CDs, PMS and visible light exhibit significant synergistic effects. In addition, lower degradation efficiency of CDs/PMS/Vis systems also suggested that Fe3+ or Fe2+ on Fe-CDs can be important active sites. Fe3+ and Fe2+ were introduced for degradation to study the difference between free and launched Fe3+/Fe2+. It can be seen that free Fe3+ and Fe2+ had almost no promoting effect for ACE degradation (Fig. 4b). Considering the low doping percentage of Fe2+ in Fe-CDs, Fe3+ anchored on Fe-CDs can be the important active site which made Fe-CDs exhibit stronger catalytic degradation performance than CDs. The Pseudo-first-order kinetic for the different oxidation processes were also calculated (Fig. S8). The kobs value of Fe-CDs/Vis/PMS was significantly higher than that of other groups, indicating that Fe-CDs had better PMS activation ability and light utilization performance.

Dosage had been studied as an important operational factor. As the introduction of Fe-CDs increases from 6 μg/L to 12 μg/L, the degradation rate of ACE continued to increase (Fig. 4c). ACE can only be degraded to 67.04 % at the dosage of 6 μg/L, which attributed to the fact that low doses of Fe-CDs failed to fully activate PMS. When the dosage reached 18 μg/L, the improvement compared to 12 μg/L dosage was not significant, indicating that 12 μg/L Fe-CDs was the optimal dosage under this condition. The concentration of PMS had also been studied (Fig. 4d). When the concentration of PMS increasing, the degradation of ACE showed a similar trend with Fe-CDs dose increasement. However, higher concentration of PMS brought slight decrease in the removal of ACE, which attributed to the excess PMS inhibiting the utilization of •OH and SO4 •− (Eq. 1−2).[48]
(1) HSO5 − + SO4 •− → SO4 2− + SO5 •− + H+
(2) HSO5 − + •OH → SO5 •− + H2O

Considering the significant impact of initial pH value in degradation and practical applications, the effect of initial pH value was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4e, with the continuous increase of initial pH value (ranging from 3 to 9), the degradation of ACE was almost unaffected. This indicated that the system can effectively degrade target contaminant over a wide pH range. To further explore the potential of practical applications, the degradation of different pollutants was also studied (Fig. 4f). Sulfamethoxazole (SMX), Bisphenol A (BPA), Tetracycline (TC), Carbamazepine (CBZ), Norfloxacin (NOR) and Ciprofloxacin (CIP) all achieved 100 % degradation with high reaction rate (Fig. 4f, Fig. S9). This indicated that the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system had excellent degradation performance for a wide range of contaminant and had practical application potential in wastewater treatment. Different oxidants (H2O2, SPC and PDS) on the Fe-CDs/Vis system had also been studied (Fig. 4g). Low degradation efficiency in Fe-CDs/SPC/Vis system (8.39 %) attributed the fact that the activation of SPC will release CO3 2−, HCO3 − and other free radicals. These anions or radicals will inhibit the iron cycle, thereby affecting the generation and concentration of the main active free radicals. Fe-CDs/H2O2/Vis system exhibited a better degradation of ACE (45.17 %). Obvious inhibition may due to H2O2 was more prone to Fenton reaction with Fe2+, the Fe3+ site on Fe-CDs cannot directly utilize H2O2 for the generation of active free radicals. The Fe-CDs/PDS/Vis system exhibited the most similar degradation performance (87.09 %) to the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system. The degradation efficiency decrease might attribute to that PMS being more easily activated compared to PDS.

To investigate the effects of various inorganic ions on the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system, different anions (Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, HCO3 −, CO3 2−) and cations (NH4 +, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+) were introduced. With the addition of different anions (10 mM), the degradation efficiency was shown in Fig. 4h. NO3 − showed slight inhibition (96.28 %) which might be attribute to that NO3 − reacts with •OH and SO4 −• to generate NO3• (Eq. 3−4) [49]. Cl− also showed slight decrease (92.85 %) in the degradation of ACE, which attributed to Cl− reacted with reactive species as quenching agents to generate free radicals such as Cl• and ClOH• with poor oxidation resistance (Eq. 5−7) [50]. In addition, Cl− can also react with PMS to inhibit the activation of PMS (Eq. 8), resulting in a slight decrease in degradation ability [51]. The slight inhibition of SO4 2− (95.80 %) was due to that SO4 2− can scavenge the generation of •OH and h+ (Eq. 9−10) [52]. HCO3 − showed strong inhibition (59.57 %) of ACE degradation, which attributed to HCO3 − was a powerful free radical scavenger that easily reacted with •OH, SO4 −•, and h+ (Eq. 11−13), producing lower oxidation potential free radicals (CO3 −•/HCO3•) [53]. HCO3 − can also react with PMS to produce less oxidizing HCO4 − (Eq. 14) [54]. The strong inhibition attributed to that •OH and h+ were the dominant species in Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system. Similar to HCO3 −, different dose of CO3 2− also exhibited strong inhibition of degradation (Fig. S10). This attributed to the reaction between •OH and CO3 2− is faster than •OH and ACE. Thus, •OH were significantly consumed and react less with ACE [55]. Compared with anions, cations did not show significant inhibition for ACE degradation (Fig. 4i). This indicated that Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis systems exhibit satisfactory degradation performance in cationic environments. In addition, humic acid (HA) had been studied as a typical dissolved organic matter (DOM) present in surface water [56]. HA exhibited a strong inhibition with 47.12 % degradation of ACE. HA may be adsorbed onto the surface of Fe-CDs, thereby preventing the contact between Fe-CDs and PMS, inhibiting the activation of PMS and severely hindering the generation of free radicals [3,57]. In addition, HA can react with •OH and SO4 −•, competing with pollutants for a large number of free radicals [4,58].
(3) NO3 − + SO4 •− → NO3• + SO4 2−
(4) NO3 − + •OH → NO3• + OH−
(5) •OH + Cl− → ClOH•
(6) ClOH• + H+ → Cl• + H2O
(7) Cl− + SO4 •− → Cl• + SO4 2−
(8) Cl− + HSO5 − → SO4 2− + ClOH•
(9) SO4 2− + •OH → SO4 •− + OH−
(10) SO4 2− + h+ → SO4 •−
(11) •OH + HCO3 − → CO3 •− + H2O
(12) SO4 •− + HCO3 − → HCO3• + SO4 2−
(13) h+ + HCO3 − → CO3 •− + H2O
(14) HCO3 − + HSO5 − → SO4 2− + HCO4 − + H+

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive species and possible ACE degradation pathways

ce:section.ce:para: Quenching experiments were conducted to identify reactive species in the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system. MeOH, IPA, NaN3, EDTA−2Na, K2Cr2O7, p−BQ, DMSO were used as scavengers for •OH and SO4 −•, •OH, 1O2, h+, e−, O2 −•, Fe (IV), respectively. Fig. 5a showed the degradation efficiency of ACE in the presence of various scavengers. EDTA−2Na greatly inhibited the reaction with almost no degradation of ACE (0.96 %), suggesting that h+ plays a dominant role in the reaction. To investigate the role of h+ in the system, the degradation efficiency of ACE at different doses of EDTA−2Na was measured (Fig. S11). The results displayed that ACE still hardly degraded even under low dose of EDTA−2Na (1 mM). Furthermore, different doses of ammonium oxalate (AO), another h+ scavenger, exhibited a similar inhibitory effect as the addition of EDTA-2Na (Fig. S12). Based on the above results, h+ was confirmed to play an important mediating role in the generation of active species. MeOH are commonly used to analyze the contributions of •OH and SO4 −• due to the similar reaction rate constant (kMeOH/•OH = 9.7×108 M−1s−1, kMeOH/SO4−• = 2.5 × 107 M−1s−1) [4]. After introducing MeOH [59], the degradation efficiency was inhibited to 27.12 %, indicating that •OH and SO4 −• play an important role in the degradation of pollutants. Because the reaction rate of IPA to •OH was much higher than that of SO4 −• [12,60]. Therefore, IPA was used to further explore the contribution of •OH. The inhibitory effect of IPA on degradation was slightly weaker than that of MeOH (29.08 %), indicating that •OH played an important role in degradation, while SO4 −• had a minor contribution in the reaction. NaN3 (k = 1×109 M−1s−1) is a classic 1O2 quencher [61]. With the addition of NaN3, the degradation efficiency decreased to 52.57 %, indicating that 1O2 also played an important role in degradation [62,63]. After adding K2Cr2O7 and p−BQ, the degradation efficiency was 61.22 % and 87.98 %, respectively. This indicated that e− and O2 −• have a certain contribution to degradation, but were not the main active species. Iron-based catalysts have been reported to generate high-valent iron species which participated in the degradation of pollutants in the PMS oxidation system [12,16,22]. High-valent species such as Fe (IV) may be generated during the activation of PMS (Eq. 13) [64]. As a scavenger for Fe (IV), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) significantly inhibited the degradation of ACE (Fig. S13). However, the reaction rate between DMSO and •OH (k = 6.60×109 M−1s−1) is faster than that of Fe (Ⅳ) (k = 1.26×105 M−1s−1) [65]. Considering the contribution of •OH represented by IPA quenching in the system, Fe (IV) species involved in degradation but with weaker generation and contribution [66]. To further determine the contribution of high-valent iron species in the oxidation process, PMSO and PMSO2 were introduced and detected. PMSO can combine with high-valent iron to form PMSO2, and the process is significantly different from other free radical reactions. Concentration changes of PMSO and PMSO2 during the reaction process were measured. Fig. 5b showed that the maximum generation of PMSO2 was only 10 μM, verifying the less contribution of high-valent iron species to ACE degradation. On the one hand, high-valent iron was a transient substance formed by rapid interaction with PMS, which was difficult to capture and react with pollutants. On the other hand, the lower generation of high-valent iron also confirmed the strong interaction between Fe-CDs and PMS, which enabled PMS to be quickly and fully activated. The strong interaction between PMS and Fe-CDs was confirmed by measuring the concentration of PMS. Compared to CDs, Fe-CDs can effectively activate and utilize PMS, resulting in a continuous decrease in PMS concentration and almost complete consumption (Fig. S14). Summarily, the contribution of •OH (λ(•OH)), SO4 −• (λ(SO4 −•)), and 1O2 (λ(1O2)) were also determined as 57.44 %, 2.32 % and 40.24 %, respectively (Fig. 5c, Text S3). These results suggested that both free radical and non-free radical pathways were involved in degradation.

To further investigate the synergistic effect of Fe-CDs and PMS on ACE degradation under visible light irradiation, the main reaction species (h+, •OH and 1O2) of Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis, CDs/PMS/Vis and PMS/Vis system were detected by ESR (Figs. 5d-5f). Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis showed much higher typical signal peak of h+ and 1O2 signal when compared with CDs/PMS/Vis system, which indicated that Fe-CDs can react with PMS to generate more 1O2 and h+ compared to CDs. It was worth noting that the capturing agent signal of TEMP-h+ became weaker as the illumination time increases, which was caused by that TEMPO generated TEMPO+ under light as a capture agent for h+. The difference in peak intensity reduction represented the intensity of h+ production. Thus, Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis generated much more h+ than CDs/PMS/Vis. Moreover, the CDs/PMS/Vis system seemed unable to continuously generate a significant amount of h+ to participate in the reaction (Fig. S15), indicating that the promoting effect of Fe-CDs on the production and utilization of h+ cannot be ignored. No characteristic DMPO−•OH, DMPO−SO4 −• and TEMP−1O2 adduct signals were detected in dark environment, which indicated that the generation of free radicals depends on photocatalytic reactions. In addition, typical peak (DMPO−•OH) with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 and a smaller shoulder peak (DMPO−SO4 −•) were confirmed in Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system (Fig. S15). However, the amount of •OH generated by CDs/PMS/Vis systems was much lower, and the signal of SO4 −• was weak and almost undetectable (Fig. S15). This indicated that the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system can generate more •OH to participate in degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible ACE degradation pathway and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Theoretical calculations based on DFT and Fukin functions were used to study the molecular structure ( Fig. 6a), active sites, and by-products of ACE. The HOMO-LUMO of ACE molecular structure were also calculated to evaluate the ability of electron-donating and electron-accepting capacity [67]. As shown in Fig. 6b, the benzene ring and its connected hydroxyl groups on ACE were most easily oxidized, followed by the −N−H bond. Fig. 6c−6e showed the f+, f−, and f0 index isosurface on ACE molecule. The higher values of atoms f0 and f−, the more likely they are to be attacked by free radicals or electrophilicity as active sites [5]. Fukui index analysis showed that 1 C, 3 C, 5 C, 6 C, and 11 O had the highest f0 values and were the most vulnerable active sites to free radical (•OH, SO4 −•) attacks in ACE (Table S3). Meanwhile, 1 C, 2 C, 4 C, 5 C, and 6 C had the highest f− values and were active sites susceptible to electrophilic attacks (1O2, h+). Notably, 6 C had high f− and f0 values, suggesting that 6 C was the most active site in the degradation process. In addition, electrostatic potential (ESP) was used to analyze the charge distribution on ACE (Fig. 6f). Higher ESP value on 12 H and 14 H suggested the nucleophilic than other regions. Lower ESP value at 16 O indicates that 16 O was more susceptible to electron donating or electrophilic attacks.

Possible transformation pathways of ACE were further studied by HPLC−MS (Text S4). During the oxidation of ACE, thirteen kinds (TP1∼TP13) of intermediates were detected from Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system (Table S4). Combined with DFT calculation of ACE molecular structure, four possible transformation pathways of ACE in Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system were shown in Fig. 6g. In pathway I, the ACE•+ radical cation was formed by the attack on the benzene ring of ACE. Subsequently, ACE•+ can react with water and generate hydroxylation products. During this process, the Fe3+ sites on Fe-CDs can also be reduced to Fe2+, accelerating the iron cycle in the system [59,67]. Subsequently, the benzene ring on TP1 decomposed to form TP2, which was further cleaved into TP3 and TP4. For pathway II, h+ can induce the self-coupling process of ACE radicals through the single electron oxidation, thereby generating dimers and trimers [5,51,62]. ACE first generated a phenoxy radical cation (ACE-OH+•) and its deprotonated phenoxy group (ACE-O•) by an OH attack, and then generated acetaminophen dimer (TP5) through covalent bond between ACE-O• hydroxy ortho carbon [68]. TP6 was generated by TP5 losing an acetamide group. Subsequently, the Namino metal chain of TP6 is oxidized into the corresponding aldehyde, thereby generating TP3. In pathway III, the ACE molecule can also be broken down by deacylation to TP9. TP9 was further oxidized to form TP10, or −OH replacement occurs on the aromatic ring of TP9 to form TP11 [4,67]. In pathway Ⅳ, 1O2 and •OH attacked the −N−H bond on ACE molecular structure to form acetic acid (TP8) and 4-aminophenol (TP12). The benzene ring on TP12 decomposed to form TP13, or the −NH2 of TP12 will be further oxidized to −OH to form benzoquinone (TP9). Afterward, degradation intermediates would be further oxidized to small aliphatic molecules and finally were converted into CO2 and H2O.

Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.) was employed to assess the toxicity of ACE and the transformation intermediates (Fig. S16). Bioaccumulation factor, mutagenicity, fathead minnow LC50, Daphnia magna LC50, oral rat LD50, and T.−pyriformis IGC50 were studied. As displayed in Fig. S16, for most intermediate products, their toxicity had been improved compared to ACE. It was worth noting that pathway Ⅱ, which degraded in the form of dimers, can ultimately form a low toxicity TP8 through ring opening reaction with a significant decrease in mutagenicity, bioaccumulation factors, and acute toxicity compared to ACE. In addition, ACE can form TP3 and TP4 with lower toxicity through pathway Ⅰ. Due to the involvement of dominant h+ in the main pathway in pathway Ⅱ, more ACE may be degraded through pathway Ⅱ, resulting in the formation of low toxicity TP8 which was further oxidized with other intermediate products into small molecule substances until oxidized to CO2 and H2O. This also indicated that the system has potential in wastewater treatment application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of h+ in mediating the generation of active species
ce:section.ce:para:
Quenching experiment indicating that h+, as the dominant active species, strongly controlled the generation of active species. In previous studies, researchers had reported cases of h+ as the highest contributing active species. Most researchers had overlooked the mediating role of h+ but focused the explanation of the mechanism on the generation of active species through electron transfer process (ETP). It was worth noting that in this study, the contribution of h+ in degradation was extremely high, suggesting that h+ can determine the production of active species in the reaction. For this study, the dominant role of h+ may have the following three pathways: 1) h+ oxidized H2O to generate •OH; 2) h+ directly oxidized ACE; 3) h+ reacted with PMS and other free radicals. For pathway 1, h+ separated from Fe-CDs cannot achieve oxidation of water to generate •OH due to the insufficient oxidation potential of VB (Eq. 15). For pathway 2, quenching results indicated that •OH and 1O2 also contribute significantly to degradation. This suggesting that h+ may mediate the generation of •OH and 1O2, and the direct oxidation pathway of ACE by h+ may not be the main degradation pathway (Eq. 16). For pathway 3, h+ can react with HSO5 − to generate SO5 −• (Eq. 17) [64]. Because of the high reaction rate of SO5 −• with itself (k = 2×108 M−1s−1) and a low activation energy (7.4 ± 2.4 kcal·mol−1), SO5 −• can rapidly and spontaneously generating 1O2 (Eq. 18) [4,64]. Phenol was used to identify the generation and contribution of SO5 −• (kSO4−• = 8.8×109 M−1s−1, k•OH = 6.6×109 M−1s−1, kSO5−• = 1.0×1010 M−1s−1) [69,70]. As shown in Fig. S17, higher inhibition confirmed the generation and contribution of SO5 −•. Then, SO4 −• can react with OH− to generate •OH (Eq. 19), ultimately forming an oxidation system dominated by non-free radical (1O2) and free radical (•OH). This also consisted with the results of the quenching experiment. Therefore, h+ can fully activate PMS to generate more active species through pathway 3. To explore the mediating role of h+ in active species, quenching experiments without visible light were also conducted (Fig. S18). In Fe-CDs/PMS system, the contribution order was •OH > O2 −• > 1O2 > SO4 •−. Compared to the lower contribution in Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system, O2 −• had a significant contribution to degradation in the Fe-CDs/PMS system. This suggested that h+ could mediate O2 −• to generate 1O2 (Eq. 20), which further confirmed the mediating role of h+. In addition, comparing the ESR signals of Fe-CDs and CDs system (Fig. 5e), the signal of 1O2 in the Fe-CDs system was significantly stronger than that in the CDs system, indicating that Fe3+ sites played an important role in the generation of 1O2. Therefore, the generation and dominance of 1O2 attributed to the collaborative of h+ and Fe3+ sites, Fe3+ sites can be an important structure for inducing h+ mediated active species generation on Fe-CDs. Nevertheless, oxygen vacancies (OVs) are also considered as important structures for the generation of h+ and the subsequent mediation of reactive oxygen species. In order to study the contribution of OVs in Fe-CDs, we conducted ESR spectroscopy. Fig. S19 showed that Fe-CDs had a g-factor value of 2.005 under both dark and light conditions. It was different from the common g-factor value of OVs (g = 2.003) and was closer to the nitrogen vacancies (g = 2.005). This result indicated the absence of OVs in Fe-CDs, which excluded the contribution of potential OVs for the generation of h+. Therefore, it can be concluded that Fe3+ sites were important active sites for achieving h+ generation and mediating the generation of active species. Overall, the synergistic effect of visible light was mainly achieved through h+, which played an important mediating role in the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system and regulated the generation of active species.
(15) h+ + H2O → H+ + •OH
(16) h+ + ACE → Oxidation products
(17) h+ + HSO5 − → SO5 −• + H+
(18) SO5 −• + SO5 −• → 2SO4 2− + 1O2
(19) SO4 −•+ OH− → SO4 2− + •OH
(20) h+ + O2 −• → 1O2

To investigate the mechanism of Fe-CDs in achieving h+ mediated active species, adsorption models of CDs and Fe-CDs for PMS were constructed by DFT calculations. The PMS oxidation pathway was hypothesized to procced through HSO5 − adsorption, HSO5 − oxidation to SO5 −• and SO5 −• desorption (Fig. S20, Fig. 7a). Specifically, the Fe3+ sites in Fe-CDs would adsorb O atom site in −SO4 of PMS, promoting the PMS oxidation to SO5 −•, simultaneously losing the H atom. The adsorption energies Eads of CDs and Fe-CDs for PMS were calculated (Fig. 7b). Compared to CDs (Eads= −1.70 eV), Fe-CDs had a higher tendency for PMS adsorption (Eads= −3.68 eV), which enabled Fe-CDs to fully activate PMS. The activation energy of CDs and Fe-CDs for activating PMS to generate SO5 −• was also calculated (Fig. 7c). Compared to CDs, Fe-CDs required lower energy to generate SO5 −•, indicating that the presence of Fe3+ sites on Fe-CDs made the generation of SO5 −• more inclined. Considering that the generation of SO5 −• was mainly mediated by h+, a stronger tendency toward SO5 −• generation suggested that Eq. 17 may be more likely to occur in the presence of Fe3+ sites. Meanwhile, density of states (DOS) was used to further investigate the mechanism of h+ mediated activation of active species. For the convenience of discussion, the Fermi level (Ef) in Fig. 7d−7e was set to 0 eV. As shown in Fig. 7e, the partial density of iron in Fe-CDs crossed the Ef, while the DOS on Ef of CDs is 0 eV, indicating the metallic properties of Fe-CDs and the semiconductor properties of CDs. Compared to DOS near the Ef, the doping of Fe significantly reduced the bandgap, and Fe orbitals were the main contributor to the shortening of CB and VB. This indicated that Fe doping can improve the light absorption ability of Fe-CDs. Moreover, Fe doping caused the Ef of Fe-CDs (Ef = −3.76 eV) to shift upward compared to CDs (Ef = −3.93 eV). This increased the concentration of photogenerated hole in the system and provided stronger impetus for the accumulation and utilization of photogenerated holes, thereby further promoting the trend of photogenerated hole mediating generation of other active species. Transient photocurrent response experiment showed that the photocurrent intensity of Fe-CDs was significantly increased compared to CDs (Fig. 7f), further confirming that Fe-CDs can better separate photogenerated carriers and also prove the increased concentration of photogenerated hole in the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system [71].

Photo-induced electrons played a vital role in the iron cycling process on Fe-CDs with Fe3+ as the active site. Fe3+ site can directly react with PMS to generate Fe2+ and SO5 −• (Eq. 21), and further generate active species such as •OH and 1O2. A portion of Fe3+ received the separated e− to form Fe2+ (Eq. 22) which subsequently react with HSO5 − to produce SO4 −• and •OH, and was oxidized to Fe3+ (Eq. 23) [58]. Fe2+ may also be oxidized by PMS to form Fe (Ⅳ) (Eq. 24) to participate in the degradation of pollutants and ultimately decay into Fe3+ and Fe2+ [72]. Fe (Ⅳ) had been proved in the above discussion (Section 3.3.1). To further confirm the reaction of PMS and Fe-CDs through ETP, the amperometric i-t curve was recorded (Fig. S21). After adding PMS, a clear jump in current density confirming the efficient electron transfer from Fe-CDs to PMS. The current density significantly decreased again after adding ACE, indicating that Fe-CDs mediated electron transfer between PMS and ACE [73]. In addition, photo-induced electrons also contributed by transferring to dissolved oxygen in water to generate O2 −• (Eq. 25). The sources of O2 −• were studied under different gas atmospheres. As displayed in Fig. 7g, degradation was almost unaffected under both O2 and Ar conditions. This indicated that Eq. 25 was not the main pathway for O2 −• generation [74].
(21) Fe3+ + e− → Fe2+
(22) Fe3+ + HSO5 − → Fe2+ + SO5 −• + H+
(23) Fe2+ + HSO5 − → Fe3+ + SO4 −• + •OH
(24) Fe2+ + HSO5 − → FeⅣ=O + SO4 2− + H+
(25) e− + O2 −• → O2 −•

In summary, possible mechanism of Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system had been proposed (Fig. 7h). We described the dominant role of h+ as the "key" to the generation of active species. Fe-CDs can easier activate PMS by h+ through Fe3+ sites to mediate the generation of active species (•OH, 1O2, O2 −•, SO4 −•, Fe (Ⅳ), etc.), ultimately forming an oxidation system with a mixture of free radical and non-free radical pathways. In the non-free radical pathway, PMS induced HSO5 − to generate SO5 −• under the induction of h+, and generated 1O2 through the self-coupling of SO5 −• as the main non-free radical active species. At the same time, the Fe3+ sites on Fe-CDs served as important sites for the generation of high-valent iron, generating Fe(Ⅳ) during the activation of PMS, further providing impetus for non-free radical pathways. In addition, e− mainly participated in the iron cycling process on Fe-CDs and the subsequent conversion of active species. The free radical pathway was mainly achieved by the further reaction of SO4 −• generated by PMS to •OH. The efficient separation and utilization of electron-hole pairs enabled the synergistic effect of Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis to be fully utilized, achieving efficient degradation of pollutants. Meanwhile, Fe3+ sites significantly enhanced the catalytic activity of photo-activated PMS, achieving the generation of active species mediated by h+.

This work reported a synthesis strategy for Fe-CDs using FG as a precursor and synthesized through a simple one-step method. Comparative experiments had shown that the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system had satisfactory synergistic catalytic performance at various conditions of pH, coexistence of cationic and anion, humic acid, pollutant type, catalyst dose, and PMS dose. Quenching experiment and ESR experiment indicated that the Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system was dominated by h+ and underwent ACE degradation through the combined action of •OH and 1O2. Based on the characterization and experimental results, the activation of HSO5 − by h+ may be the primary determining step for the generation of •OH and 1O2. DFT calculation analysis results indicated that the dominant role of h+ was attributed to the Fe3+ site on Fe-CDs, which led to a lower activation energy for PMS compared to CDs, thereby promoting full contact between h+ and PMS. The Fe3+ sites on Fe-CDs efficiently activated PMS by forming an efficient iron cycle by photo-induced electron under visible light irradiation. The Fe-CDs/PMS/Vis system proposed in this work is an effective organic pollutant degradation process, and provides new insights for efficient activation of PMS and h+ dominated photocatalytic processes.
